The shield, with serrated edges and dark shading, bears two crescents in chief, a fesse (charged with what appears to be a label or band) across the middle, and a crescent in base. Above the shield is a wreath topped by a small boar (passant). Decorative mantling spreads to either side and an empty banderole curves across the base. Below the shield, in blackletter: 'Fitz Simons.'

Milliken at his death was seized of a tract of land in Berkeley County known as Castle Hugh Tract, lying between the Northeastern Railroad Track and a Pineland Settlement, with Joe Joiner living thereon under an agreement for purchase. Milliken consisted of (a) a tract of land in Williamsburg County between the Lanes and Salters stations on the Northeastern Railroad (containing 178 acres more or less), (b) a one-half interest in the Castle Hugh Tract, and (c) a Plantation tract of land on Cooper River known as North Mulberry. notes: Continuation of the multi-page manuscript letter from pages 261–262. Written in brown ink in a more open hand than 262 (largely horizontal, not cross-hatched), making this page substantially more legible. The text is a deathbed scene: the dying woman tells Mrs. Northrop that she is willing to die and wishes to be baptised by the Priest; Mrs. Northrop and the Priest succeed in clearing the room; the dying woman declares 'I do it only on his account I am unchanged and die in the faith of my forefathers and you must say so'; Mr. Walker objects to her baptism but she takes his hand and turns her wasted face to him, saying 'I say it is only right to Christian as he thought I could not go to Heaven unless Baptised but it is only to make him happy'. The writer reports a long conversation with Mrs. Walker about being a Protestant, his trust in God's mercy for pardon, and concern for the children: 'Yes tell Mother that I have thought of this continually since she left & throughout my illness with intense affection; tell her that I thought of her in my dying moments, gave her my love.' Signed 'Yours aff'l'y / W Huger FitzSimons.' Some words and figures uncertain because of typewriter strikeover, the dim ribbon, and pencilled annotations along the right margin (apparently 'Gaillard's' [box?], 'Huger's letter,' and other notes whose exact wording is uncertain).

notes: Typewritten letter dated September 12th, 1918, addressed to 'Mr Thomas E. Osborne, Horse Shoe, N.C.' from W. Huger FitzSimons. Conveys the terms on which his sister (Ellen B. FitzSimons) is willing to sell her Mills River Farm — referring back to her letter of January 16th, 1917: fifteen thousand five hundred ($15,500.00) dollars for the Farm, not including the Mountain land which she wishes to reserve; $5000.00 cash and balance ($10,500.00) in five equal successive annual installments of $2100.00 each with interest on all unpaid credit portion at six (6%) per cent per annum payable annually; advises Mr Osborne to wait for sister's confirmation but expects to get the rent for this year. He had written to Santor (or Sauter) from Hendersonville and explains that he had promised to mention Mr Osborne and would shortly be in Charleston. Signed 'Sincerely yours,' (typed name not visible). Below the typescript a handwritten note in blue ink reads: 'Dear Sister: / Above is a copy of my letter / to Mr Osborne of this date. / [initial flourish — possibly 'H.J.G.' or similar] / Sept 12/18.' Several details in the typewritten body are uncertain because of strikeover and a faded ribbon.

The hand is loose cursive in brown ink on plain stationery; the page appears to have been mounted to the album near its upper edge with the lower edge free. Sender unsigned on this sheet — context (date, references to 'home,' 'Dad,' and 'mother,' and overseas mail delays) is consistent with a WWI letter written from France.

P.' on the following sheets) has had heart trouble and the doctors have advised him to slow down; while Sam and Janice were visiting in Panama City, J. P. asked Sam if he would consider going to work for him with a view to eventually buying out the business. Pairs with pages 372 and 373.

people: Sam FitzSimons (Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons, III); Mary H. FitzSimons; Janice FitzSimons
notes: Second sheet of the letter begun on page 371, blue-ink cursive on cream paper. Sam continues with the details of his prospective new job with Janice's father: that it is to be straight business — he will work for one year and then, if both parties are satisfied, begin buying him out, an arrangement that may take 10 to 20 years; that Janice's sister is equally entitled to her share; that his salary will start around $10,000 a year; that he and Janice will be happier living in Panama City; and that he has told his current employer he is leaving in three weeks.

people: Louisa FitzSimons
notes: A single sheet of letter paper (recto), written in dark brown ink in a confident, looping hand, headed 'Halifax, N. S. / Jan. 15th. 1918.' The salutation is 'Dear Louisa:—'. The writer reports having just returned to Halifax after an eight-day cruise to Labrador during which the ship encountered gales and blizzards with temperatures of 25° below zero, the vessel becoming a solid mass of ice; the crew of a wrecked schooner was rescued. The writer mentions sending a previous note that, if it reached Louisa, will have travelled over 400 miles by dog sled. The page ends mid-paragraph; the letter continues on page 446. The paper is heavily foxed and the centre fold has begun to split. The signature is on the second sheet (page 446). The Halifax dateline, the Labrador cruise, the ship locked in ice, and the rescue of a schooner's crew strongly suggest a U. S. Navy or U. S. Coast Guard officer writing during the First World War; identity not certain but consistent with a male FitzSimons cousin of the recipient Louisa.

people: Louisa FitzSimons
notes: Continuation of the letter begun on page 445. The scan shows two leaves of the same letter: at the foot, the verso of the first sheet (continued in the same dark ink, the writing oriented normally and running across the lower half); at the top, the second sheet of the letter laid above it, with its writing oriented upside-down relative to the lower portion (so that when the first leaf is turned over, the second leaf reads right-way up). The writer concludes with news that they will move from Halifax up the coast of Labrador for the rest of the winter, that the ship is going into weather still worse than what they have just been through, and signs off with thanks for Louisa's letter and the comforting news that the family is well. The signature appears to read 'Brother' followed by an initial or short name; not confidently legible. The letter as a whole describes a U.S. Navy or Coast Guard winter patrol cruise during the First World War.

The body recounts the writer's chance meeting in a French street with Mr. FitzSimons's son — 'our aviator' — at first not recognising him until the son hailed him with 'Don't you remember Major B—?' The writer praises the young man's spirit and reports they dined together and that the writer went to the aviator's airfield the following afternoon. The 'aviator' is almost certainly Gavin FitzSimons, son of W. Huger FitzSimons. The letter continues onto another page (not present).

It identifies him as a nephew of Major Theodore G. Barker (one of his mother Susan Milliken Barker's brothers) and praises his fidelity to Charleston and to the people of South Carolina.

Huger FitzSimons himself appears to be a slip — the letter on 484 is dated September 8, 1939, the year W. Huger died, and addresses 'Miss FitzSimons' (his sister Ellen) about her brother. Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons was indeed W. Huger's brother, but his death significantly predates 1939; the brother whose loss this 1939 condolence letter actually mourns is W. notes: A densely composed memorial album page for William Huger FitzSimons and his wife Annie Cain. At the top left, a cabinet-card portrait of the young W. H. FitzSimons is captioned beneath in pencilled block capitals. Top right is an oval bust portrait of Annie Cain in a lace collar with a long cross pendant. Across the center top, the compiler has inked the parents' birth and death dates and listed their five children in a hand-drawn box. The middle row holds three oval portraits of the eldest sons — James Cain, William Huger Jr., and Samuel Gaillard. Below sits a half-length portrait of Marguerite FitzSimons captioned with her marriage to Dr. Robert Preston, beside a small color snapshot of Reginald FitzSimons in later life. To the right, a Hendersonville newspaper clipping under the headline 'Henderson Man Still Wants to Fight Germans' reports Reginald's 1942 enlistment in the Canadian army at age 42, alongside a marginal annotation listing the three sons of Anne Preston and Alexander Worth (Alexander Walker Worth, David McAlister Worth, Robert Preston Worth). The left margin carries a long biographical narrative of W. Huger FitzSimons's life and legal career, written in the compiler's hand.

notes: An album page assembled around items connected with Ellen Milliken FitzSimons. Upper left, a carte de visite of a young woman in a long fringed dress, captioned at the top edge in faint cursive 'Mamschen Darlingchen' and beneath the print 'Lina Shoppaul – 20 years –'; beneath the card, in another hand, the caption '\"My German governess.\" E.M.F.' Beside the photograph hangs a large yellow manila pocket whose printing 'INTERNATIONAL WORLD WHO'S WHO / 240 BROADWAY, NEW YORK 7, N.Y.' is faintly visible through the paper (it appears to be the original mailing pocket for the Who's Who proof sheet mounted on page 489). A small handwritten label inside the page next to the pocket lists 'Letters from to Ellie / John F.S. / Louisa F.S. / Ellen F.S. / Frank F.S.' indicating that letters from these correspondents were once kept in the pocket. Beside it is a folded sheet of notes summarising 'Copy of Resolutions of Board of Trustees of the Charleston Library Society given to Ellen M. FitzSimons when she retired — Also copy of her reply — Letter from Mr. Harrison Randolph - Pres. [Co]llege of Charleston telling Ellie that she had been chosen to receive an honorary degree of Doctor of Laws.' Down the right side, three pasted-in envelopes addressed to Ellen at different addresses (Spartanburg 1909, Charleston 1905, and forwarded to Mrs. J. Pickens Walker at Jacksonville 1948).

notes: A single sheet of buff letter-sized paper printed as a press-proof form by 'INTERNATIONAL WORLD WHO'S WHO, 240 BROADWAY, NEW YORK 7, N.Y.' for the 1948-49 edition. The form is headed 'PROOF SHEET' and bears the standard instruction to the biographee to avoid corrections. Pasted into the lower-centre rectangle of the form is a small white slip — the actual galley A5 entry for FITZ SIMONS, Ellen Milliken, LL.D., librarian of the Charleston Library Society — listing her birth at Mulberry Plantation, schooling, publications, and home address at 4 Savage St., Charleston. A footnoted asterisk explains that her LL.D. was conferred (not earned) by the College of Charleston. A red-ink overprint near the bottom of the form urges the recipient to return the corrected proof within a week.

people: Ellen Milliken FitzSimons; Mr. Ingle
notes: A single sheet of typed letter — apparently a carbon copy or the writer's file copy — on thin onion-skin paper, the right-hand edge of which is clipped off the page so that the last three or four characters of each line are missing. Dated May 27, 1948 and addressed to the Board of Trustees of the Charleston Library Society. The letter is Ellen Milliken FitzSimons's reply to the trustees' resolutions of appreciation on her retirement after fifty years as Librarian. She accepts the role of Honorary Librarian and adds a brief P.S. addressed to a 'Mr. Ingle' who delivered the resolution to her. Unsigned in this copy. Companion piece to the resolutions and the College of Charleston honorary-degree letter referenced on page 488.

people: Theodore G. Barker; Ellen Milliken FitzSimons; Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons
notes: A single sheet of letter paper containing the closing page (apparently page 2 or 3) of a longer letter, written in a sloping cursive in dark ink. The body of the letter implores the recipient and a relative referred to as 'Gaillie' (i.e., her brother Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons) to visit, asks her to wire when she will arrive at the train, and is signed 'Yrs affectionately / Theodore G. Barker' followed by the address line 'Miss Ellen M. FitzSimons / Spartanburg.' Above the body is a sentence-fragment marked with a caret and a marginal insertion '(At my Expense (of course))' showing that the writer offered to pay for Ellen's telegram. Letter relates closely to the Adams Run letter of Oct. 12, 1905 on pages 492–494, in which the writer Minnie P. FitzSimons refers to having read 'Uncle Theodore's letter to Sam.'

people: Minnie P. FitzSimons; Theodore G. Barker; Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons
notes: First page of a three-page letter (continues on pages 493 and 494) written from Adams Run, S.C., October 12, 1905, by Minnie P. FitzSimons to 'My dear Aunt Louisa.' Written in dark ink in a sloping cursive on a single sheet, head-down. Minnie has just read a letter from 'Uncle Theodore' (presumably Theodore G. Barker, whose letter to Ellen at Spartanburg is mounted on page 491) addressed to 'Sam' — her husband or relative Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons — and is shaken by news of the imminent death of a beloved elderly woman who has been 'a Mother to me since I came among you.' The corresponding envelope, addressed to Miss Ellen M. FitzSimons in care of the Charleston Library (struck through) and re-directed to 131 Tradd Street, Charleston, is mounted on page 488 — the letter was apparently delivered to Ellen at the Tradd Street address where Aunt Louisa was living.

people: Minnie P. FitzSimons; Ellen Milliken FitzSimons; Theodore G. Barker; Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons
notes: Middle page of the three-page letter from Minnie P. FitzSimons to Aunt Louisa, dated October 12, 1905 from Adams Run, S.C. Shown as the two facing leaves of an opened folded letter sheet — the left-hand leaf is the continuation of page 492 (a tribute to the dying woman's character), and the right-hand leaf records Minnie being called from her writing desk to the telephone, where 'Dear Ellen' herself rang to tell her the news had come — i.e., the elderly woman has died. The letter pivots here from being a forward-looking message to Aunt Louisa about the news in 'Uncle Theodore's letter to Sam' into a direct condolence addressed to Ellen. Continues on page 494.

people: Minnie P. FitzSimons; Ellen Milliken FitzSimons; Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons
notes: Closing page of the three-page letter (begun on 492, continued on 493) from Minnie P. FitzSimons at Adams Run to her Aunt Louisa, October 12, 1905. Minnie reflects on a recent summer visit, mentions that her baby may resemble the deceased, and expresses condolences directly to Ellen, whom she affirms was the 'center of her life, her joy and pride.' She is glad that 'Sam' will be with Ellen while the funeral arrangements proceed. Signed 'Affectionately yours, Minnie P. Fitz Simons.'

people: W. P.
notes: A single sheet of cream wove paper covered in cursive in blue or gray ink. No salutation visible at the top — the page begins mid-sentence and is one of several pages of a longer philosophical letter (continued on page 500 and apparently page 502). The writer reflects on people who are 'apt to have brains, imaginations and some pretty strong feelings,' the limitations of the 'Philistine,' and references a 'W. P.' — possibly W. P. Trent or another correspondent. Handwriting and tone resemble the philosophical-literary voice elsewhere in the album. A small tear and stains affect the right edge.

A second sheet of the same letter as page 499, in the same hand and ink. The writer continues philosophical reflections, addressing the recipient as 'you' and praising her sensitive nature. References 'Miss J.' (possibly Miss Jenkins or Miss Johnston) and 'the Welsh' or 'Celts.' Page is creased and stained. Continued on page 502.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons
notes: A typed (typewriter) resolution on a single sheet, framed as a series of three 'RESOLVED' clauses, recognizing fifty years of service by Miss Ellen M. Fitz Simons as Librarian of the Charleston Library Society, and providing for her retirement at full pay effective October 1, 1948, the title of Honorary Librarian, and a silver testimonial. The page is a duplicate or carbon presentation copy and shows fold-creases. Spelling is as typed — 'Fitz Simons' is two words throughout.

Third sheet of the same letter as pages 499–500, in the same hand and ink. The writer concludes reflections on Philistinism and shares a wish to take the recipient to a quiet village in England or a chalet in Switzerland to talk of fathers and mothers. Lower half of the page is ruled but blank, suggesting the letter ends here (with no signature visible on this sheet — the signature may be on a separate sheet not preserved).

A single sheet of cream paper written in pencil cursive, deeply creased and partially faded. The page begins mid-thought and is one of several philosophical letter pages preserved in this part of the album. The writer reflects on American society — that 'apptitude' (aptitude) tends to count for everything; that institutional excellence is not admired; that what is wanted is a man or woman who fits smoothly into the machinery rather than an interesting individual; and that the 'uplift' movement, magazine pictures and Sunday-paper illustrations only stimulate the superficial sensations. The text continues onto the next leaf but the connection to subsequent pages is unclear. Page is folded in half vertically; ink is faded and several words remain uncertain.

Ellie writes about the autumn color in the North Carolina mountains and recalls earlier visits to Mills River. The letter continues on pages 506–508 and is signed 'Affectionately, Ellie' on page 508. 'Ellie' is Ellen M. FitzSimons, the Charleston librarian recently retired (see page 501); the recipient — addressed as 'Sister' — is Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker), the album's compiler.

She mentions that 'The Waterfalls' have returned to Columbia, and that 'Hail and George' (probably nicknames within the family) have developed a real liking for country life. 'Savage Street' is a street in downtown Charleston. The page is in blue ink on the same lined paper as page 505.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Margarita FitzSimons; Frank FitzSimons; Aunt Lil
notes: Third sheet of Ellie's letter of October 26, 1948 (continued from page 506), numbered '3' at the top. Ellie continues with thoughts on country life and reports that 'Margarita' (perhaps Margarita FitzSimons) need not worry about her three babies — Frank, Aunt Lil and Ellie herself — whom she left behind. She remarks that the family is keeping the peace so that the new baby can be enjoyed, but adds that 'babies will be babies' and they cannot be expected to get along indefinitely without their nurse, nor to keep jealousy of the little 'Bo' baby from developing.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)

The bottom half of the page is blank ruled lines.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; J. Harold Easterby
notes: First sheet of a two-page letter in blue ink on lined paper, addressed 'Dear Mr. Easterby' and signed 'Ellie & Mr. Easterby' (the closing/inscription on page 510). The writer thanks Easterby for sending his book and acknowledges what a great opportunity it was to make an outstanding contribution to South Carolina history. Ellie inserts interlineated corrections — 'scholar' above 'ability,' 'documents' above 'materials,' and 'indexing' above 'arrangement'. The recipient is almost certainly J. Harold Easterby (1898–1960), Charleston historian and director of the South Carolina Archives, author of A History of the College of Charleston (1935) and editor of The Journal of the Commons House of Assembly. Continues on page 510.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; J. Harold Easterby; Nathaniel Wright Stephenson
notes: Second and final sheet of Ellie's letter to Mr. Easterby (continued from page 509). She concludes praising the book as 'an incontrovertible testimony to the much misunderstood & often maligned character of our people & civilization,' and expresses pleasure that the Charleston Library Society, the South Carolina Historical Society and the College of Charleston are associated with a work of this magnitude. She wishes that 'Nathaniel Wright Stephenson could have lived to rejoice in it'; Stephenson (1867–1935) was a Charleston-connected historian and professor at the College of Charleston, author of works on Abraham Lincoln and the Old South. The closing/inscription at the foot of the page reads 'Ellie & Mr. Easterby' (the album-compiler's later notation identifying the correspondents). The middle of the page is blank ruled lines.

notes: First page of a letter written in dark ink on a single sheet, dated 'Hendersonville N.C. / Wednesday April 1, 1925.' Addressed to 'Dear Sister.' Mentions a request for an 'up-to-date photograph' for the New York Camera Club of Theodore Jervis, and reflects on the progress made by 'Doctor' in two years of medical care, now able to walk with dignity. Closing portion mentions Hugh Barnwell, Frank, and other family members. The hand is loose and crosses itself in places; several words are uncertain. Letter continues on page 514.

A pencilled docket near the bottom reads 'Uncle Sate & Ellie' identifying the correspondents in the compiler's later hand. Signature 'Affectionately / S.P.S.' (or similar — written across the page) precedes the postscript. Same loose hand as page 513; several phrases uncertain.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; Minnie; John; Maggie
places: Charleston, South Carolina; Hendersonville, North Carolina
notes: First page of a letter in blue ink on a single sheet, dated only 'Monday.' Addressed to 'My dearest Ellie,' apparently from a sister or close relative writing the day after a celebration in Ellie's honor — perhaps the retirement dinner referred to elsewhere in this section. The writer regrets having had to leave without staying to speak to Ellie; recounts that Minnie had telephoned the previous night to describe the celebration ('it was all in your honor & centered around you & that you looked grand!'); reports that John comes through on his way to Hendersonville at 7 p.m. the following night, that the writer will meet him at the station for 25 minutes, then come home and catch the night train to Charleston (but not the night one) and get back Friday morning. Asks for details of the retirement, hopes the agonising-over details will be relieved, and expresses concern that Ellie should not have to 'drag back & forth this summer.' Asks 'What are they going to do for a librarian?' Closes with love to the family. Continues on page 516.

FitzSimons (older woman in spectacles and a beaded necklace, looking past the camera) is captioned '—Photo by Marion / ELLEN M. FITZSIMONS.' Beneath, a strip of body text — the lower portion of a longer article — describes eight of the twenty men and women who are to receive honorary doctor's degrees from the College of Charleston at its sesqui-centennial celebration, May 12–14. The college was founded in 1785, so the sesqui-centennial places this clipping in May 1935. The clipping is trimmed; the left edges of each line have been clipped off (initial letter or two of each line missing). The Justice Van Devanter mentioned is Willis Van Devanter (1859–1941), Associate Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court 1910–1937.

notes: A typewritten letter on plain paper, single sheet, much faded with carbon-purple ribbon ink. Dated 'Hendersonville, N.C. / March 26th, 1935' at upper right. Addressed 'Dear Sister:-' — i.e., to Ellen M. FitzSimons, the Charleston Library Society librarian. Signed at lower right with a small handwritten monogram in blue ink (the initials are not fully resolved on the scan — possibly 'W.H.F.' for William Huger FitzSimons, who is listed at page 504 as one of the brothers whose letters follow in this section). The letter thanks Ellen for letters and books, discusses Springtime in Charleston, refers to John's recent news from the South Carolina end, comments on family obligations and recent deaths (Julia Hammond, the George Moffett 'tragedy'), and encloses $5.00 for Library dues.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; Theodore Barker
notes: First page of a handwritten letter in blue fountain-pen ink on a single fold of cream notepaper, dated April 23, 1943. Addressed 'Dearest aunt Ellen' — to Ellen M. FitzSimons, the Charleston Library Society librarian. The writer signs herself 'Ellen' on the closing leaf (page 526) and is the same niece who writes to 'Aunt Ellen' on pages 527 and 530–531 — i.e., Ellen Jr. The writer hopes the letter will reach Aunt Ellen on Easter Sunday and apologizes for not having written sooner after the death of Theodore Barker.

notes: The inside spread of the April 23, 1943 letter begun on page 525 — two leaves laid side by side as the bifolium opens. The right-hand leaf (page 2 of the letter) continues directly from page 525 with the writer's account of a soldier, Mr. Bernard Ryan, who came twice to the Charleston Library Society asking to meet her — Aunt Ellen's niece — because he so admired Aunt Ellen. The left-hand leaf (page 3) continues that anecdote — quoting Ryan's praise of Miss Ellen Fitz Simons — and turns to Easter plans involving 'Nip, James and family,' 'the Old Man,' and possibly Frankie coming home. The closing of the letter is not on this page (likely on the fourth side of the bifolium, not photographed here, or on a separate sheet).

FitzSimons, the Charleston Library Society librarian). The writer signs herself 'Ellen' (a niece) and mentions Easter church services, a theatre outing for Libby's birthday, and plans to come to Charleston in August on vacation. Gibbs, President (presumably of the Library Society board), with the date '1963' written by hand beneath the signature.

FitzSimons, the Charleston Library Society librarian) and could hardly wait to relate how delightful and keen-minded they found her. notes: Second page of the handwritten letter begun on page 530, dated 13 July 1948 to Aunt Ellen (Ellen M. FitzSimons). The writer reflects on the professional respect and personal affection between Aunt Ellen and Virginia Rugheimer, mentions a letter from 'Louisa' enclosing a discerning article from the *News and Courier*, and another letter from 'The Old Man' confirming the news of Virginia's appointment. She then recounts a hot summer dinner with Emily O'Reilly while Tom was in Philadelphia covering the political convention.

notes: Typed letter on letterhead 'Henderson County / Hendersonville, N. C. / Office of Register of Deeds / Frank L. FitzSimons, Registrar / Marshall Watterson, Deputy.' Dated 14 March 1944. To 'Dear Aunt Ellen' (Ellen M. FitzSimons of Charleston), from her nephew Frank L. FitzSimons. He shares news from his three children in military service during the Second World War: Reginald (recovering after the Sicily, Britain, and Italian campaigns); Frankie (aboard a PT boat in the Pacific, addressed care of Fleet Post Office, San Francisco); and Margie (location not stated). Mentions 'Uncle Tote' growing weaker. Signed in ink, illegibly but evidently 'Frank L. FitzSimons.' Closes affectionately, with the word 'after' inserted by hand into one line.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; Aunt Louisa
places: Hendersonville, North Carolina; Brookland; Charleston, South Carolina
notes: Single sheet, handwritten in brown/sepia ink in an elderly, careful hand. Dated 'Hendersonville N.C. November 9, 1909' and addressed 'My dear Ellen.' The writer (apparently an older uncle of Ellen M. FitzSimons — possibly Frank Williamson FitzSimons or another senior relative, signature not present on this sheet) urges Ellen to come over to Hendersonville and Brookland before going down to Charleston, observing wistfully that he and 'your Aunt Louisa' are growing older and cannot be sure she will find them there another year. Letter continues on a sheet not preserved here.

people: Ellen M. FitzSimons; Virginia Rugheimer
places: Charleston, South Carolina; Charleston Library Society
notes: Album page densely covered with newspaper clippings and small photographs documenting Ellen M. FitzSimons's long tenure as librarian of the Charleston Library Society, her retirement, and her death on 9 July 1955. At upper left, a small oval photograph and a handwritten ink note in the compiler's hand giving Ellen's vital dates: born March 27, 1862; died July 9, 1955. The dominant clipping at the top is a feature article 'Librarian Forty Years for Venerable Book Society — Here is Called Charleston's Cyclopedia,' illustrated with a head-and-shoulders portrait of Miss FitzSimons. At lower left, a clipping with portrait headed 'Miss FitzSimons's Retirement Will Mark the End of an Era,' by-lined Mary B. McLeod. To the right of that, three columns: 'Deaths and Funerals — Miss Ellen M. FitzSimons, Librarian At Library Society for 30 Years, Dies,' 'Miss FitzSimons To Be Buried This Morning,' and '30 Years With Library.' A small 'News Briefs' clipping appears below. At upper right are two photographs: Ellen at her desk in the library, and the Charleston Library Society building. The page is annotated in pencil and ink throughout.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)
notes: Single sheet, written in dark blue fountain-pen ink on lined cream paper. Dated Spartanburg, S.C., June 16, 1940. Begins 'Dear Amy,' with a wry meditation on growing old and using age as an excuse for one's faults. The page ends mid-sentence; the continuation is not preserved with the album page (no further sheets of this letter are mounted on pages 537–542, which contain a separate later letter). Signature is not visible on this surviving sheet.

people: Ellen Mikell FitzSimons; Henry C. Hammond
places: Augusta, Georgia; Black Mountain, North Carolina
notes: First sheet of a six-page humorous letter (pages 537–542), numbered 1–6 in pencil at the top of each sheet. Written in dark blue fountain-pen ink on cream paper. Dated '12/ Oct. 40' and opening 'My dearest Ellen.' The writer is almost certainly Judge Henry C. Hammond of Augusta, Ga. (partner in Hammond, Kennedy & Yow, whose envelope is mounted on page 535). He describes, with mock-solemn comedy, attending the funeral of another woman named Ellen who has been mistaken for the recipient, and the genealogical 'skirmish' that broke out among the mourners over which Ellen had actually died. The letter is a tongue-in-cheek love-letter affirming the living Ellen's place in the family of Christopher and Catherine FitzSimons.

people: Ellen Mikell FitzSimons; Henry C. Hammond
notes: Second sheet of the six-page Judge Hammond letter (pages 537–542), numbered '2' in pencil at the top. The mock-elegiac comedy continues: he protests the Presbyterian officiant on the grounds that the dead Ellen was Episcopalian.

people: Ellen Mikell FitzSimons; Henry C. Hammond
notes: Third sheet of the six-page Judge Hammond letter (pages 537–542), numbered '3' in pencil. The writer claims to the undertaker and monument man that the deceased was wealthy ('Texas oil lands and stocks and bonds in excess of a hundred thousand dollars') and identifies himself, when challenged, as 'Graham from Black Mountain.'

people: Ellen Mikell FitzSimons; Henry C. Hammond
notes: Fourth sheet of the six-page Judge Hammond letter (pages 537–542), numbered '4' in pencil. He claims to have summoned 'Kit, Mary and their numberless children' (other FitzSimons kin) to the graveside, where the mourners rushed from tomb to tomb reading inscriptions and protesting that the recipient was in fact alive.

people: Ellen Mikell FitzSimons; Henry C. Hammond; Christopher FitzSimons (the emigrant)
notes: Fifth sheet of the six-page Judge Hammond letter (pages 537–542), numbered '5' in pencil. The argument over identities resolves into a genealogical 'skirmish' over whether the writer's and recipient's shared great-grandfather Christopher FitzSimons had a brother from whom the dead Ellen was descended.

people: Ellen Mikell FitzSimons; Henry C. Hammond; Christopher FitzSimons (the emigrant); Catherine Pritchard FitzSimons
notes: Sixth sheet of the six-page Judge Hammond letter (pages 537–542), numbered '6' in pencil at the top. The writer denies any such brother and asserts that the great Christopher and Catherine FitzSimons were 'the parents and grand parents of all those who slept in the Cottage Cemetery,' and asks the recipient to settle the question. The page ends mid-sentence; the closing and signature are on additional sheet(s) not preserved with this album opening. 'Cottage Cemetery' is the family burial ground in Charleston that the FitzSimons surrounded with cottages, often referenced in this album.

people: Henry C. Hammond; Ellen FitzSimons
notes: First of two pages of a letter in old cursive on yellowed paper, written by Henry C. Hammond to his cousin Miss Ellen FitzSimons of Charleston, South Carolina (the signature and address appear on page 544). Page numbered '1' at top center. Text begins mid-thought, suggesting an earlier sheet may have been lost. The letter recounts the visit of 'the Grahams' on their way to Charleston to see their son, and the writer's gift of a camellia plant from 'the emperor of Japan' intended as a token of devotion.

people: Henry C. Hammond; Ellen FitzSimons
notes: Concluding page of a letter on yellowed paper, signed Henry C. Hammond and addressed at the bottom 'Miss Ellen Fitz Simons / Charleston / South Carolina.' Page numbered '2' at top. The closing anecdote recounts a humorous exchange about presenting flowers intended for one Ellen to 'the real Ellen.' The signer Henry C. Hammond is likely a connection of the Hammond branch — see Catherine FitzSimons (Mrs. Hammond) elsewhere in the album.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)
notes: Second page (numbered '2' at top center) of a multi-page letter to Amy. Page 1 is not present in the album; pages 546 and 547 continue and conclude the letter, signed 'G.S.F.' The writer congratulates Amy on 'Dee's marriage' to a doctor, drawing a parallel with the writer's own grandfather who was 'a doctor and proved himself to be a fine husband and father.' Continues with a promise to send information about 'our family.'

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Hugen FitzSimons; Ellen FitzSimons
notes: Third page (numbered '3') of the letter signed G.S.F. The writer mentions sending Amy a 'memorandum of the FitzSimons family' compiled by 'your Uncle Hugen' — i.e., the family-history document that is the cornerstone of this album. The writer also defers to 'Aunt Ellen' as the keeper of family memory and hopes to get her to write down what she knows.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Theodore FitzSimons; Clara FitzSimons; Frank FitzSimons
notes: Fourth and final page (numbered '4') of the letter to Amy, closing with 'Affectionately, G.S.F.' The writer recounts a Sunday visit to 'After Glow' with 'Theodore, Clara and their children; also Frank and his family,' and reflects that he expects to settle permanently at After Glow within weeks. The initials G.S.F. (likely a FitzSimons) and the family circle described place this in the mid-twentieth century.

The writer confirms that Amy returned the 'Memorandum of the Fitz Simons family' (referenced in the preceding letter), reports on 'Aunt Ellen' — eighty years old that month — who broke a month's vacation to visit Charleston via Columbia, lunching with the Waterfalls and visiting the C. Fitz Simonses.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Ellen FitzSimons; Frank FitzSimons; Pickens Walker
places: Hendersonville, North Carolina; Henderson County, North Carolina; East Flat Rock, North Carolina
notes: Second page of the Nov. 10, 1942 letter. Reports on 'Uncle Tote' (in feeble health, soon to be 85), on Frank's recent appointment and election as Register of Deeds for Henderson County (filling an unexpired term and then a full four-year term), and on Frank's resignation from the principalship of the East Flat Rock School. Frank is also trying to obtain a military commission; his age, false teeth, and other obstacles have so far prevented him.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Pickens Walker; Frank FitzSimons Jr.; Reg FitzSimons
notes: Final page of the Nov. 10, 1942 letter, signed G.S.F. Reports on Pickens's military rejection for defective vision, on 'Frank Jr.' (about to turn 18) attending Marshall College at Huntington, W.Va. and planning to leave college in January to volunteer for the Marines, and on cousin 'Reg,' still with the Canadian forces in England and now possibly at the front. Closes with affectionate reflections on Amy as a young grandmother.

A single sepia-toned snapshot mounted on an otherwise blank album page. A young dark-haired woman in a dark wool suit and corsage sits on a built-in bench at the end of a wooden dock, smiling toward the camera. Behind her on the railing are two beer bottles and a small glass; an open handbag rests on the bench at her left. A wide tidal river or estuary is visible behind, with a dim treeline on the far shore. No caption or date is written on the page. The figure is unidentified but the print's vintage and tonality are consistent with the late 1940s FitzSimons family snapshots in the surrounding pages.

A single sepia-toned studio portrait mounted on an otherwise blank album page. Head-and-shoulders view of a young woman with short wavy reddish-brown hair, hoop earrings, and a white open-collared blouse. She smiles slightly toward the camera. The print shows minor surface scratches and a hairline crease at the upper right. No caption or date is written on the page. The figure is unidentified; the style of the portrait and the woman's apparent age suggest a date in the mid-to-late 1940s, consistent with other portraits of the FitzSimons women in this section of the album.

A single sepia-toned snapshot mounted on an otherwise blank album page. An elderly bald man in a light summer suit, bow tie, and round spectacles stands beside a young woman in a pale short-sleeved dress and a dark cloche-style hat, holding a small bouquet of white flowers. They stand on the lawn in front of a porch with white-painted columns and a low hedge of azalea or boxwood behind them. The mood is formal, possibly a wedding or anniversary snapshot. No caption or date is written on the page. The pairing of an older relative and a young bride or graduate is consistent with mid-1930s FitzSimons family events documented elsewhere in the album.

A single faded color snapshot mounted on an otherwise blank album page. Two small boys stand side by side on a patchy lawn before a hedge of yellow forsythia in early bloom. The boy at the left wears a dark blue T-shirt, light jeans, and dark shoes; the boy at the right wears a green-and-yellow striped T-shirt, dark pants, and light shoes. A small black dog sits at the feet of the left-hand boy. Beyond the forsythia, leafless trees and the corner of a frame house are just visible at the upper center. The photograph has the faded magenta cast characteristic of early-1960s color prints. No caption or date is written on the page. The boys are unidentified but are likely Walker or Allston grandchildren of the compiler's generation.

Single color snapshot mounted on a blank album page. Photo lab date along the right margin reads 'FEB . 65.' Four figures pose in formal evening dress before a fireplace and a portrait in oils. Left: a young man in white tie and tails. Center-left: a middle-aged woman in a blue satin gown with long white gloves and a pearl choker, holding a small corsage. Center-right: a young woman in a pale satin gown with long white gloves. Right: a young man in white tie and tails. The setting appears to be a private home with a panelled fireplace, candlesticks on the mantel, and an Oriental rug. No handwritten caption is visible on the page.

Single color snapshot mounted on a blank album page. A toddler in a white pullover sits cross-legged on the seat of a large, ornately carved Victorian-style armchair upholstered in a floral brocade. A small framed needlework picture leans against the chair at left, and a framed landscape hangs on the wall above. No date or caption is visible on the page or photo border.

Single color snapshot mounted on a blank album page. Photo lab date along the right margin reads 'AUG . 70.' A wedding party of six stands at the altar of a church before stained-glass windows and a flower-decked altar with lit candles. Left to right: a young man in a tan suit and dark tie; a woman in a red dress and red hat holding a bouquet (likely the mother of the bride); the bride in a long white gown holding a bouquet; three young men in dark blue blazers, grey trousers, dark ties, and white boutonnieres (groom and groomsmen). No handwritten caption is visible on the page.

Single color snapshot mounted on a blank album page. Two young women in floor-length evening gowns and long white gloves stand in an interior. The woman at center wears a pale blue empire-waist gown with beaded bodice and earrings; she stands beside a small side table with a potted plant in a silver bowl. A woman at the right edge of the frame, partly cropped, wears a darker patterned gown and rests her gloved hand on a carved wooden chair. Behind them are a draped window at left and a tall wooden mantel or cabinet with a candle lamp at right. No handwritten caption is visible on the page.

notes: A large folded leaf, photographed open so that several panels show side by side, some inverted, with bleed-through visible from the verso. The page is densely written in a 19th-century hand in iron-gall ink. The legible right-side-up columns at the lower half of the sheet appear to form the opening of a numbered legal-political essay or opinion arguing against the constitutionality of the Reconstruction Acts of 1867 — specifically that Andrew Johnson's Amnesty Proclamation and the existing State governments cannot be overturned by Congressional action without subverting law. Only the right-side-up portions are transcribed here; the upper panels (inverted or partially obscured by the fold) are noted as illegible at this scan resolution. Continues on page 652.

people: Robert E. Lee; Joseph E. Johnston
notes: Continuation of the essay begun on page 651. Same folded leaf opened to display further columns; the upper portions show bleed-through and the lower-left and lower-centre columns are right-side-up and largely legible. The text argues, as a question of Law (not politics), that the Military Reconstruction Act of 2 March 1867 ('the Act to provide more efficient governments for the Rebel States') and its supplementary Act would overthrow the existing State Constitutions and impair the rights of citizens as guaranteed by the U.S. Constitution. Cites the surrender of Lee and Johnston in April 1865 as the moment when Civil Law was restored. Most likely a contemporary copy of a Reconstruction-era legal opinion or argument.

people: Becky (nurse); Theodore Barker; Dad; Mammie; Lafayette; Charley
places: Sandersville, North Carolina; Rock Spring; Edisto; Pon Pon
notes: First page of a long handwritten reminiscence in blue ink on lined paper, evidently by one of the compiler's family. A small carte-de-visite photographic portrait of an elderly Black woman in a white headwrap is tipped onto the lower left of the page and captioned 'Becky' — the family nurse described in the text. The narrative recounts the writer's earliest memories: a christening in a church in Sandersville, N.C.; the nurse Becky; and the move with Dad and 'Mammie' to Rock Spring where Theodore Barker planted rice.

people: Theodore Barker; Becky (nurse); Rosa; Dad; Mammie; Brownie
notes: Continuation of the handwritten memoir on lined paper. Describes Uncle Theodore Barker buying land on the mainland from Mr. Greenwall and building a one-story frame house at Rock Spring. The writer recalls Becky setting a lamp and the house catching fire, and Aunt Rosa's wedding at Rock Spring at which the writer (a child) was an attendant in a white silk dress.

people: Brownie; Alice; Mammie; Dad
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Recalls a hollow oak tree where the Brownies lived, a cousin who said he saw 'the Flute' in the Brownie tree, the 'Jubilee Wading of Alice through the Looking Glass' kept in a closet under the stair with a bayonet, and Mammie's flower garden — roses, flowering almond, and Germain Iris. The writer recounts being whipped by Mammie for the first time after stripping leaves from a young pear tree.

people: Mammie; Dad; Uncle Kit; Theodore Barker
places: Rock Spring; Mount Hope; Oak Hurst; Gibbes; Rosemont; Shoengaussen
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Describes Dad buying Shoengaussen plantation, Uncle Kit buying Mt. Hope, Oak Hurst, and Gibbes, and the move from Rock Spring to Mt. Hope. The writer's only memory of the move is being carried across the river by Mammie wearing a big apron and bearing a basket — and a cowbell tied around the writer's neck so Mammie could keep track of her.

people: Oak Hurst; Mr. Barnwell
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Describes the Oak Hurst garden, which had been burned during the Civil War — the family story that Northern soldiers came to Oak Hurst, Mr. Barnwell refused to come out, the officer ordered the men away rather than burn him in his chair, and the garden survived in part: japonica, sweet shrub, azalea bush, and fields of jonquils, snowdrops, and narcissus that bloomed amid the ruins of the Gibbes house.

people: Dad; Mammie; Hunkin; Bull
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Records Dad ploughing Mt. Hope in 1893 for the first time — a 'disastrous' year on record because of the August 1893 hurricane. The writer recalls the storm rolling the rice schooner 'Storm' so far inland it was wedged on the hill, and notes the contented happy years that followed. The page ends with the beginning of an account of Hunkin's illness and death — the children sharing a room, Bull running to call Daddy for the doctor, the diagnosis of 'membranous croup' (diphtheria).

people: Hunkin; Dad; Mammie; Bull; Becky (nurse); Hannah
notes: Continuation of the memoir, recording Hunkin's death from membranous croup (diphtheria) on the early morning of February 4th — the writer's birthday. Describes Mammie sitting wrapped in a gray flannel wrapper, feather-stitched in blue wool, dark hair piled high — not letting her grief conquer her, calm and quiet for the sake of the other children. The page closes recalling the nursery and Becky being the first to come, then Mammie Hannah.

people: Mammie; Dad
notes: Continuation of the memoir, numbered '2' at the upper left corner of the page (the writer's own pagination). Recalls the nurses' fairness — never having a nurse who favored one child over another — the nursery windows with begonias, geraniums, Roman hyacinths, and a few sweet pea seeds on each end of the box, vines trained up the sides — and Dad rising early to build roaring fires in his room and the nursery before 7 o'clock so the nurses could come up, bathe the children, and dress them in time for the family breakfast at a certain hour.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Mauma Judy; Ellen
notes: A page of handwritten memoir in blue ink on lined paper, mid-narrative. The writer (the compiler, Amy FitzSimons) recalls nursery routines on the plantation: a bird-catching incident with Mauma Judy, the laundering of baby clothes, the constant fire in the nursery, and the brewing of tea on the hearth. Continued from the previous page.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Mauma
notes: Continuation of the same handwritten memoir, blue ink on lined paper. Describes the evening anointing of children with melted mutton tallow, the heavy white canton flannel 'night drawers' or sleeping garments, the nurse dozing by the fire until the children slept, and the writer's conviction that nursery rules made for a happier home life.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Had
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Describes the formal late-afternoon descent to the living room for supper, the children's grandfather 'Had' who sat by the fire telling stories and called the writer 'Gabey,' and the grief of the household at his funeral when the negroes came to pay their respects.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); William Gonzales; Aunt Lil; Uncle Willie
notes: Continuation of the memoir. The colored friends come to pay last respects to 'boss and friend Mister Simons.' Mr. William Gonzales, editor of the Columbia State and 1st ambassador to Peru, visits in winter dressed for the cold and tells the writer wonderful stories. Aunt Lil and her children also visit; Uncle Willie was a cotton buyer.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Aunt Lil; Emily (Mrs. Charles Russell); Quitlie (Mrs. Tom White); Aunt Louisa; Mr. Kershaw
places: Charleston, South Carolina; Camden, South Carolina
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Aunt Lil decides to teach the children to dance; the Buenois children — including Emily (now Mrs. Charles Russell), Sonnax, and Quitlie (now Mrs. Tom White) — come over from the Parsonage. The writer recalls having measles in Charleston and being unable to see her hands. Mr. Kershaw, rector of St. Michael's church in Charleston, is introduced as a dear family friend.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Mr. Kershaw; Mrs. Kershaw; Aunt Helen Porcher; Cousin Annie Waring; Cousin Janie Waring Aiken; Cousin Gaius Aiken; Bob Aiken; Glencoe Aiken
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Mr. Kershaw came hunting each winter and was a great favorite. Mrs. Kershaw came too — described as 'a fussy, fat little lady, always full of stories about Charleston queens.' The writer recalls the front porch of the plantation house where Mauma Mim and her friends sat to talk and where the children listened to Mrs. Kershaw, Aunt Helen Porcher, and Cousin Annie Waring. Cousin Janie Waring Aiken and her husband Gaius are noted; their sons Bob and Glencoe went into the world after their father's death.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Dr. Grimball; Dr. Taylor; Uncle Hit; Aunt Sammy; John; Louisa; Aunt Helen Porcher; Dr. William Thomas Berry
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Dr. Grimball took care of the family on the plantation, later Dr. Taylor. Uncle Hit made regular visits and always brought presents. John and Louisa came with Aunt Helen Porcher; John was amused on his first visit by a cow asking 'Does it walk through the house?' The writer reflects on her mother's full life as mistress of a plantation — required to be artist, author, notable — and to settle plantation arguments. Dr. William Thomas Berry is mentioned as one who married a girl and locked up his nation and refused to fish her.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)
notes: Continuation of the memoir. Describes the 'medicine closet' off the dining room — always locked, holding household remedies and Dr. Tell the Dr. Comes. All the darkies on the plantation came to 'the house' for doctoring; if beyond Mauma Mim's care, the patient was put in a buggy and driven six miles to the doctor in Adams Run village. The writer recalls a man brought to the yard with a badly cut foot whom Mauma Mim treated to stop bleeding before sending him on. Hog butchering in cold weather, with 8 to 10 hogs killed and Had supervising the cutting up in the cellar.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Dad; Eulalie Barnwell; Theodore Roosevelt; Lucille Johnson; Andrew Sasson; Uncle Theodore Barker
notes: Handwritten page in blue ink on ruled album paper, continuing the long first-person memoir of Amy FitzSimons. Recounts the annual sausage-making at Rock Spring (with 'colored women' cutting the meat, lard rendered in the kitchen, and gifts of products to relatives and friends in Charleston); the governesses who came when school time arrived — Miss Eulalie Barnwell (a 2nd cousin of Theodore Roosevelt), then Miss Lucille Johnson; and the writer's recollection of Andrew Sasson living at Rock Spring planting for Uncle Theodore.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Andrew Sasson; Carrie Sasson; Judith Sasson; Annie Sasson; George Warren; Lichut Warren; Dad; Uncle Theodore Barker; Aunt Jennie; Buck
places: Prospect Hill; Rock Spring; Charleston, South Carolina
notes: Handwritten page in blue ink on ruled album paper, continuing the memoir. The Sasson family (3 daughters — Carrie, Judith and Annie — and cousins George and Lichut Warren from Prospect Hill) attended school at Rock Spring with the writer. Recollections of the plantation breakfast table — hominy, sausage, hog's head cheese, scrambled eggs, biscuits and waffles or muffins, and game salmi. The writer's amusement at recalling Uncle Theodore's bewildered guest 'Buck' who was offered sausage at every meal. Children were never allowed to leave the plantation after the 3rd of May because of malarial fever; the writer first went to Charleston for school at the 1st of May.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Uncle Theodore Barker; Aunt Louisa Barker; Aunt Ellen Porcher; Dad; Gibson Brown; John; Louisa
places: North Carolina; Charleston, South Carolina; Brookland
notes: Handwritten page in blue ink on ruled album paper, continuing the memoir. The family went up to the mountains in N.C. through the summer until schools opened in the fall; the Sasso school always opened the 1st Monday in Oct. If the baby was on the way, the writer spent the season in Charleston at Uncle Theodore's house, 121 South St., and Aunt Louisa went up to 'Brookland' about April. Aunt Ellen Porcher (Uncle Theodore's widowed sister, who lived with them) brought John and Louisa up. Dad would come to Charleston every morning, bringing a hamper of vegetables — butter — eggs and chickens. He left early in the morning. That local train was called the 'Planters Special' for all the rice planters commuted on it during the summer. A colored man — Gibson Brown — ran a livery stable and he sent horses around every morning to pick up the planters and take them to the train — and brought them home in the evening.

She mentions a recent visit by 'Miss Emmie' who passed through Spartanburg without their knowing it, and reports family news: the baby trying to walk and talk, a new black kitten, and an afternoon helping the people pick cotton.

She sends love to Miss Kate and Hettie. The lower half of the sheet is blank, with faint horizontal rulings. The word 'Friday' is struck through and replaced.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Mother; Father; Mammie; Gladys; Phillip (the cat); Uncle Theodore Barker; Aunt Ellen Porcher
notes: Handwritten page in blue ink on ruled album paper, continuing Amy FitzSimons's memoir. people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons
notes: A blank pre-printed check from the Carolina Savings Bank of Charleston, S.C., signed in advance by Minnie P. FitzSimons but with payee, date, and amount left empty. This is almost certainly the 'signed blank check' described in her letter of April 5, 1933 (pages 094–096), enclosed for her daughter Amy to use if Minnie should fall seriously ill or die. The check bears the printed routing fraction 67-5/5 at upper right and the bank's circular monogram seal as a background watermark.

people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)
notes: First sheet of a three-page letter written in black ink on cream stationery, dated only 'Wednesday. July 14th' with no year. Signed 'Mother' on the third sheet (page 093). The hand, signature, and references to 'Brother', 'papa', and Bingamon (a FitzSimons family connection or property) identify the writer as Minnie Perry FitzSimons writing to her daughter Amy. Mentions that the letter is being written on her [sister Susan]'s birthday — most likely her half-sister Mary Susan Perry named in the family memorandum on page 031.

people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Lil; Mr. Ambler
notes: Continuation of the 'Wednesday, July 14th' letter begun on page 091. The writer describes a trip to Charleston to see the dentist, accompanied by 'Brother', a visitor's prospective purchase of Bingamon, the arrival of Lil and her husband, and the recent departure of Mr. Ambler after a week's visit. 'Bingamon' is the writer's spelling — likely a family place name; original spelling preserved.

people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); James Pickens Walker; Jessica; Minnie; Nin; Clara; Julia
notes: Third and final sheet of the 'Wednesday, July 14th' letter (pages 091–093). The lower half of the sheet is blank. Several words in the news paragraph are difficult to resolve and are marked uncertain or illegible.

people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); James Pickens Walker
notes: First sheet of a three-page letter in blue ink on cream stationery, dated 'Wednesday. April 5th 1933.' Signed 'Mother' on the third sheet (page 096). This is the letter that arrived in the Yonges Island envelope on page 089, addressed to Mrs. J. P. Walker in Savannah; the body refers to the writer's recent illness ('my Indian sickness') in Savannah and encloses a signed blank check (the Carolina Savings Bank check pasted on page 090). Salutation reads 'Dear Amy'.

people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons; Theodore FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)
notes: Second sheet of the April 5, 1933 letter (pages 094–096), numbered '2' in the upper left corner. The writer explains the enclosed signed blank check (the Carolina Savings Bank check on page 090): if she dies or again becomes incapacitated, her daughter is to use the check to draw out her bank balance and pay Theodore — likely her son — eighty dollars a month.

people: Minnie Perry FitzSimons; Theodore FitzSimons; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Sam Jr.; Mary; William Moffett
notes: Third and final sheet of the April 5, 1933 letter (pages 094–096), photographed as a bifolium showing two columns side by side — page 3 at left (numbered '3' in the upper left corner) and page 4 at right. The writer continues her instructions to her daughter Amy about how to pay Theodore eighty dollars a month from the enclosed signed check, then turns to family news: the birth of Sam Jr. to 'Sister and Mary' (reading uncertain — possibly 'Sister and Sam' have a son named for his father), the recent death of William Moffett, and the writer's enjoyment of Amy's son who is visiting. Closes 'Devotedly, Mother,' with a postscript that she is feeling better than before her illness.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); James Pickens Walker Jr.
notes: Second leaf of a multi-page letter written in brown ink in a small slanted cursive hand on cream paper, mounted as a single sheet on the album page. The letter begins on page 104 ('Dear little Amie') and concludes on page 106 (signed 'Mother', dated 'Friday'). The mailing envelope is on page 107, postmarked Yonges Island, S.C., May 27, 1926. 'Your boy' refers to Amy's son James Pickens Walker Jr., who would have been about fourteen.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); James Pickens Walker Jr.; Mr. Anderson; Mrs. Anderson
notes: Third and final leaf of a multi-page letter in brown ink on cream paper, signed 'Mother' and dated 'Friday'. The hand is small, slanted, and difficult to decipher; many words are uncertain. The letter continues from pages 104–105 and was mailed in the envelope on page 107 (postmarked Yonges Island, S.C., May 27, 1926). The closing reference to a young bride 'not young, ordinary' but who 'will probably make him a good and useful wife' completes the thought begun on page 105 about the niece marrying her 'chosen lord'.

people: Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)

notes: A cream envelope mounted on the album page, franked with a 2-cent red Washington stamp and a circular postmark reading 'YONGES ISLAND, S.C. / MAY 27 / 4 30 PM / 1926'. Killer bars to the right of the postmark. Addressed in brown ink in the same hand as the letter on pages 104–106. The mailed letter from Amy's mother — Amy Perry FitzSimons (the elder) — was sent from Yonges Island, where the family kept the Fair View plantation.

places: Fair View, Yonges Island, South Carolina
notes: A single black-and-white snapshot mounted at the center of the album page. The page bears no caption. The photograph shows a one-and-a-half-story frame house with a deep screened porch supported by slender columns, two gabled dormers in the metal roof, and live oaks draped with Spanish moss at left and right. Plantings of low shrubs run along the foundation; bare sandy ground in the foreground. The print is contemporary with the photographs of garden and grounds on pages 109–112 and likely shows Fair View, the FitzSimons plantation on Yonges Island referenced in the 1926 letter from Mother (pages 104–106) and its envelope postmarked at Yonges Island (page 107).

places: Fair View, Yonges Island, South Carolina
notes: A single small black-and-white snapshot mounted on the album page, with no caption. The view shows a sandy garden enclosure planted with tall flower spikes (likely larkspur or hollyhock), a square stone or concrete pedestal at center bearing an urn, and a broken earthenware jar lying on its side on the ground at lower right. Outbuildings — a smaller cottage and what appears to be a privy or shed — are visible through the live oaks in the background. Companion image to the house and grounds on pages 108 and 110–112, all likely Fair View on Yonges Island.

places: Fair View, Yonges Island, South Carolina

A round sundial on a low pedestal stands in the right foreground beside a deep border of flowers — larkspur, foxglove, and other tall blooms — running the length of the porch. The same house appears head-on in the photograph on page 108. Likely Fair View on Yonges Island.

A house wall and porch rail are visible at the right edge. No caption is visible on the print.

The image shows the side of a two-story wood-frame plantation house seen at an oblique angle through the trunks of two enormous live oaks draped in Spanish moss; a dark touring automobile is parked beside the house and two chickens forage on the lawn at the right. An outbuilding is visible at the far right. No caption is visible on the print.

notes: A loose-leaf lined-paper album page with a handwritten memoir entry in blue ink above and below a sepia photograph mounted at center. The photograph shows three figures seated at a heavy round library table: a young woman at left in a dark dress, an older bearded man at center reading by a hurricane lamp, and a girl at right; a globe stands on the table and bookshelves line the wall behind. Continues Amy FitzSimons's narrative of family moves with the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad — Jacksonville from June 1923, Savannah from 1926 — and the family's summers at Black Mountain, N.C. and Camp Carolina in Brevard.

Album page with a single large cabinet-card photograph mounted at center. The card is a sepia studio portrait of a bald, round-faced infant in a long white gown, seated upright in a floral-upholstered armchair. The studio imprint is printed in dark ink across the lower margin of the card mount. No handwritten caption appears on this page; the subject is unidentified. The Clarksburg, West Virginia origin is unusual for this album — most of the family material is from South Carolina, Georgia, and Florida.

people: James Pickens Walker Jr.; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); James Pickens Walker
notes: Album page with three pieces of mail ephemera mounted across the sheet. An empty business-size envelope at the upper right is annotated across its back in blue ink. A second envelope at lower left, brown with age and torn at the upper edge, is annotated on its back panel in blue ink. At lower right, a folded brown paper telegram wrapper or envelope is annotated in blue ink down its length. The annotations identify each piece as a letter or telegram from 'Pickens', presumably James Pickens Walker Jr., addressed to or about his parents 'W. & S.' — most likely an abbreviation for Walker and (FitzSimons) or for the parents' household initials.
